data.title: Opendoor: Upgrading To A Speculative Buy
data.description:
When Opendoor Technologies Inc. (OPEN) stock was making waves last year, I thought the company’s valuation made little sense. Last September, I highlighted 2 reasons to sell OPEN stock and never look back. I thought Opendoor’s core business was deteriorating behind the curtains. I also believed its capital-light pivot will not address profitability challenges for quite some time. Since then, OPEN stock has declined by over 50%. A couple of months later, in November 2025, I published another article discussing why the housing market decoupling story told by Opendoor’s new CEO fails to capture the whole truth, as this new strategy forces the company to rely on transaction volume to secure growth.

After an evaluation of the macro outlook for the real estate sector, I am changing my stance on OPEN stock as I believe it satisfies my conditions to qualify as a high beta play on the expected recovery of the housing market.

Institutional Sentiment Is Shifting

The U.S. real estate sector has gone through a lot of pain in the past few years. Several reasons have contributed to these challenges, including persistently elevated mortgage rates (peak rates of around 8% in 2023), a surge in home prices that limited affordability, and inventory shortages that led to a housing shortage of anywhere between 2 million and 5 million units, depending on the data source.

These challenges limited institutional investments in the real estate sector as a whole in the past few years. Today, interestingly, institutional sentiment seems to be shifting in favor of a recovery.

Recent macro studies and published research reports signal that a recovery of the real estate sector is on the cards this year. According to PERE’s Perspectives Study for 2026, 45% of institutional investors are willing to deploy more capital into real estate investments this year compared to 2025. According to PERE, this would be the highest level of capital deployment into the real estate sector in more than a decade. This high allocation into the real estate sector is expected to stem from the significant levels of underinvestment in real estate over the past few years (according to PERE, 63% of institutional investors are underweight in their real estate allocations).

Cohen & Steers (CNS) also believes that 2026 will be a strong year for the real estate sector. According to their estimates, REIT and public real estate earnings growth will accelerate to around 8% this year and will remain at a similar level in 2027 as well. Cohen & Steers expects listed real estate to outperform private real estate this year, which highlights the growing appetite for public real estate among institutional investors.

Exhibit 1: 2026 forecast returns for listed and private real estate

After reading several market reports by CBRE, Cohen & Steers, the National Association of Realtors, and JP Morgan, I have found a few reasons that point to a recovery of the real estate sector in 2026.

  1. CBRE forecasts softening labor market conditions and cooling inflation will lead the Fed to cut rates this year.
  2. If rate cut expectations materialize, mortgage rates will decline. This will expand the pool of residential real estate buyers. According to NAR, a 1% decline in mortgage rates will boost the number of households eligible for a mortgage by 5.5 million.
  3. CBRE projects commercial real estate investments to increase 16% YoY to $562 billion this year, representing a full recovery from the pandemic slowdown.
  4. Continued AI investments will boost the demand for certain types of real estate (data centers, for example).
  5. Housing inventory is rising, improving the affordability of homes (according to NAR, housing inventory is up 20% YoY).

If you are a commercial real estate investor, I strongly recommend reading the 5-page report published by Blackstone’s Global Head of Real Estate, Nadeem Meghji, a couple of weeks ago, where he builds a strong case to take a contrarian view and invest in this sector today.

Some institutional investors seem to be bottom-fishing already. A Schedule 13G filing last November showed that D.E. Shaw has accumulated just over 60 million OPEN shares, representing an ownership stake of 6.4%.

The Case For OPEN As A High Beta Bet On A Real Estate Recovery

Before we proceed further, let me remind you that I was bearish on the prospects for OPEN not so long ago. There are many investment vehicles that could give me decent exposure to the real estate sector to benefit from a potential recovery of this sector this year. For me to think of OPEN as one, which turns me into a bull, definitely took some doing. There are a few main reasons behind my changed stance.

First, Opendoor’s inventory clean-up has made the company far more investable than it was a few quarters ago. Given the macro pressures facing Opendoor, these improvements have hardly had a say on its stock price movements in the recent past. However, if macro fortunes turn for the better in 2026, I believe Opendoor’s balance sheet cleanup will be rewarded handsomely. According to Q3 2025 filings, Opendoor’s real estate inventory stood at $1.05 billion as of September 30. For context, by the end of 2024, inventory stood at $2.15 billion. For those who are not familiar with Opendoor’s struggles, the problem with this massive inventory pile was the high prices at which it was accumulated.

Exhibit 2: Opendoor’s real estate inventory

Now that the inventory cleanup is underway, whatever new inventory that hits Opendoor’s balance sheet is likely to be recorded at much more reasonable valuation levels. The inventory base, therefore, is of much higher quality compared to the past.

Second, Opendoor’s potential to benefit from operating leverage makes it a great speculative stock pick when revenue growth starts trending higher. Under the new CEO Kaz Nejatian, Opendoor is aggressively cutting costs. These are part of the Opendoor 2.0 strategy. AI automation is likely to play a big role in these cost cuts as well. If the real estate sector recovers this year, transaction volumes will grow inevitably. When this happens, incremental revenue growth will see a notable improvement compared to the past, given that Opendoor’s fixed cost base will be much lower. This will set the stage up for a strong comeback for OPEN stock.

Third, as I highlighted in my previous article, Opendoor will be dependent on transaction velocity to drive earnings higher. In a macro environment where transaction volume growth is more likely than not within the next 12 months, this business model shift will serve Opendoor well. Currently, Opendoor’s contribution margins are very thin (2.2% in Q3 2025). This is bad. What is encouraging is that laser-thin contribution margins will see a massive boost even if clearance rates improve slightly. Even a slight reduction in days on the market could turn out to be a massive boost to contribution margins. The bar that Opendoor has to clear is quite low. This makes OPEN stock a good candidate as a high beta play on the expected recovery of the real estate sector this year.

Finally, a revival in housing market activity will help Opendoor to break even from an adjusted net income perspective this year, meeting the original timeline projected by the company. Mr. Market will reward this outcome.

Q4 Earnings Preview

Opendoor is expected to report Q4 2025 earnings on February 19. Consensus estimates point to $594 million in revenue for Q4, representing a 45% YoY decline. The loss per share is expected at 11 cents compared to 14 cents in the corresponding quarter of the previous year. This improvement is expected on the back of a major improvement in the company’s cost profile.

The real highlight of the upcoming earnings release would be the management’s commentary. I will keep a close eye on a few important discussions and developments.

  1. The outlook for home acquisition (the management has guided for a notable sequential home acquisition growth for Q4).
  2. An update on the path toward achieving breakeven adjusted EBITDA.
  3. The outcome of the first-ever Financial Open House, where the management will address the questions of retail investors (questions will be pulled from Robinhood’s Say Technologies platform).

Risks

Opendoor is still a broken business in my view. Although I believe OPEN stock has the potential to deliver handsome rewards as a high-beta play on the expected recovery of the real estate sector, this doesn’t mean that Opendoor has suddenly transformed into a much better business. A lot still depends on a macro recovery, so Opendoor is far from being out of the woods. Any adverse developments from here on could end up wiping out shareholders. In addition, most of Opendoor’s stated growth plans rely on integrating AI tools successfully into its business processes. This is easier said than done. And we still have no idea about the challenges the company will face while doing this. I expect the management to shed more color on these initiatives next week when the company reports earnings.

Takeaway

I am changing my stance on Opendoor stock. Not because I believe the company has transformed into a much better business, but because I believe it has the qualities I look for in a high-beta investment opportunity to make the most of macro improvements. If the real estate sector recovers as expected this year, I believe OPEN stock will outperform the market and its sector peers, given how a slight macro improvement could result in a notable improvement in financial performance.

data.title: IYZ: Telecommunication ETF With Little Competition
data.description:
Fast facts about the iShares US Telecommunications ETF

The iShares US Telecommunications ETF (IYZ) was launched on May 22, 2000 and tracks the Russell 1000 Telecommunications RIC 22.5/45 Capped Index. IYZ has a portfolio of 22 stocks, a 30-day SEC yield of 1.70% and an expense ratio of 0.38%. Distributions are paid quarterly. It is a mid-size ETF with sufficient liquidity for investors and traders as well, with $768 million of AUM (assets under management) and an average daily dollar volume of $53 million. The fund’s issuer iShares is a part of Blackrock (BLK), with over 1,500 ETFs and $4.2 trillion of AUM globally.

IYZ's Strategy

IYZ started in May 2000 tracking the Dow Jones U.S. Telecommunications Index, then switched to the Russell 1000 Telecommunications RIC 22.5/45 Capped Index on September 20, 2021. The current underlying index targets the companies of the Russell 1000 Index classified in the Telecommunications sector by FTSE Russell. It includes three sub-sectors: Telecommunications Equipment, Cable Television Services and Telecommunications Services. The index is rebalanced quarterly and weighted based on float-adjusted market capitalization, with a maximum of 22.5% in any issuer and 45% in aggregate in issuers individually exceeding 4.5%. The fund’s turnover rate was 30% in the most recent fiscal year.

FTSE Russell’s ICB sector classification differ from GICS, which is used by many ETFs, making comparisons and benchmarking difficult. Indeed, the Telecommunication Equipment industry is included in the Technology sector by GICS, while internet companies like Alphabet (GOOGL) and Meta Platforms (META) are classified in Technology by ICB. Therefore, it makes little sense to use a communication benchmark such as the Communication Services Select Sector SPDR® ETF (XLC) to assess IYZ.

Portfolio

About 63% of asset value is in large companies, 28% in mid caps and 9% in small and micro caps. The GICS Communication Services sector weighs 61%, and Information Technology 39%. At the industry level, the portfolio has a focus on Communication Equipment (41%) and Integrated Telecommunication Services (32%).

The portfolio is very concentrated, with significant company-specific risk. The top 10 issuers, listed in the next table, represent 74% of asset value, with Cisco Systems over 20%, and Verizon and AT&T at about 12%.

Nonetheless, its equal-weighted peer XTL has a better track record since 2011 and lower company-specific risk.

  • Cons: underwhelming historical returns, underperforms XTL.

data.title: Taking Advantage Of Xiaomi's Consolidation - Top 2026 Long Pick
data.description:
The Chinese tech giant Xiaomi has been facing partial hiccups lately that made a share price correction understandable. Nevertheless, my thesis is that the drop represents exactly that: a correction during an obviously non-linear company expansion after Xiaomi had previously shattered early expectations. As expected, 2025 EV sales, with a total of around 411,000, far exceeded the management’s target of 350,000 as well as my own expectations. However, 2026 guidance of 550,000 EVs (+34% YoY) is now disappointing to some, while it does not at all represent structural headwinds to Xiaomi’s broader expansion story. The intact long-term trajectory paired with a significant valuation correction makes Xiaomi one of my top 2026 long picks. Let us break it down.

We Can Shift Away From The Smartphone Narrative

During Q3-25, Xiaomi reported 22% YoY revenue growth, 37% gross profit growth, and 150% operating profit growth. Topline growth was driven by tripling EV sales, while smartphone sales were down 3%. EVs have surged to become the company’s largest gross profit contributor, while IoT and lifestyle product margins also expanded notably, and Internet services maintained their extraordinarily high 77% gross profit margins. Smartphones, again, were the soft spot with declining margins. On a company level, operating leverage on expanding EV output nevertheless helped almost double operating margins from 7% to a strong 13%.

First EV Sprint Successful, Scaling Next

With EV sales having surged quarter after quarter during 2025, Xiaomi has had an enormous kickstart in its second year in this new field. Delivery numbers had shattered management’s own communicated forecasts and my very own estimates. While delightful, any growth story—especially into competitive markets—is unlikely to play out linearly. Therefore, it does not at all damage the mid- to long-term story that management is now guiding for a “softer” delivery number of 550,000 EVs during 2026. This would still imply 34% YoY growth, and it must be noted that with this number, Xiaomi is still only acting within the boundaries of its Chinese home market. International expansion into Europe is expected for 2027, and peers like BYD have proven that Chinese manufacturers can succeed in the West despite automotive being one of Europe’s legacy industries. The 2026 growth slowdown is partly attributed to the reduction of Chinese tax support accompanying some pull-forward demand in 2025.

Looking at the Chinese EV sales landscape during December 2025 below, Xiaomi continued to almost double its sales YoY, although this means a growth slowdown at a very high level. With that, Xiaomi still represented only 3% of domestic volume share for the month, leaving ample headroom to gain from competitors. In fact, leaders BYD, VW, and Toyota continued to crumble against smaller contenders in December.

Are Smartphone Headwinds That Important?

If anything could be of serious concern for Xiaomi, it is its latest global smartphone market share trends. According to IDC, during Q4-25, for which Xiaomi itself has not yet reported numbers, the company saw a significant YoY reduction in smartphone shipments of 11%, while both Apple and Samsung accelerated their shipment growth. In terms of market share, Xiaomi is thus losing significantly to Apple and Samsung. Remember that this was not yet reflected in the previously discussed Q3-25 smartphone revenues having been down 3%, while Q3-25 shipments were still up 2% YoY. The -11% Q4-25 shipments number by IDC therefore braces investors for a very bad YoY smartphone revenue and profit reading in the upcoming earnings report, representing another likely reason for recent share price weakness.

To take stock, Xiaomi faces enormous headwinds in its smartphone segment, albeit out of a position of global leadership (#3) and strength. With the latter being said, it would be premature to classify these temporary headwinds as structural.

Furthermore, while it might still appear to some as if smartphone shipments were the company's make-or-break drivers, they have only contributed 20% to the company’s gross profit during Q3-25, while 25% to 28% each come from the growing and margin-expanding IoT and lifestyle products segment, the cash-printing Internet services segment capitalizing on the company’s already vast user base, and the surging new EV segment. Against this backdrop, I also sense that widely discussed increases in input costs due to memory chip shortages will have a manageable impact on company margins.

Summing this up, it appears that the recent share price drop has mainly factored in smartphone weakness and the normalization in the EV buildout, while none of the company’s broader long-term growth and profitability narratives appear endangered.

After A Year In Excess: Back Below Mean

On a TTM basis, Xiaomi reported $5.2 billion in free cash flow, according to Seeking Alpha. This is despite a doubling in capex spending to $2.9 billion, up from $1.4 billion a quarter prior. Put that in perspective to the company’s now notably compressed enterprise value of $112 billion to land at an undemanding 4.6% free cash flow yield or 22x free cash flow multiple. Undemanding in the sense that Xiaomi is a company that has proven to grow into the global Top 3 of the competitive smartphone market and has proven to grow relevant in the competitive EV market at record speed. Plus, Xiaomi manages to do all that while decreasing leverage and thus increasing its net cash position to almost record levels.

Furthermore, with an adjusted TTM P/E of 21x, Xiaomi has now landed below its own long-term average of 24x—a valuation level last seen sustainably during 2024.

2026: A Year For Consolidation Before Global Expansion

Xiaomi stock’s weak H2-25 price performance has priced in the potentially bumpy year 2026 the company is heading into, while also correcting the valuation premium it has earned through its notable EV kickstart. Over the course of 2026, however, investors might be eyeing 2027 and years thereafter again, which I suspect will be shaped by the EV segment’s international expansion reaccelerating the segment’s growth. At the same time, Xiaomi can handle temporary smartphone headwinds, as the segment only contributes 20% to gross profits within a vast ecosystem of higher-margin products and services than smartphones. Finally, valuation appears to now fully reflect the short- to mid-term challenges ahead, allowing for favorable long-term entry points into this Chinese tech ecosystem and growth powerhouse, making it one of my top 2026 long picks.

Editor's Note: This article was submitted as part of Seeking Alpha's Top 2026 Long Idea investment competition, which runs through February 28. With cash prizes, this competition -- open to all analysts -- is one you don't want to miss. If you are interested in becoming an analyst and taking part in the competition, click here to find out more and submit your article today!

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: CALM Chain International Seeks IPO At Excessive Valuation And High Risks
data.description:
CALM Chain International is Growing, But Risks Are High

CALM Chain International Limited (CCIL) has filed to raise $18 million in an IPO of its Class A ordinary shares, per an F-1 registration statement.

The company provides a range of human resource outsourcing services in Hong Kong.

CCIL is growing but has an uneven operating history, high risks, thin capitalization, and excessive valuation assumptions at IPO.

My outlook is to sell (avoid) the CCIL IPO.

What Does CALM Chain Do?

CCIL operates as Talent Quest in Hong Kong and was founded in 2012 to provide various human resource outsourcing services primarily focused on finding and retaining qualified employees for small and medium-sized businesses in the greater Hong Kong region.

The company is led by founder, Chairman, and CEO Mr. Andrew Teh Yao, who is also a venture partner and mentor at Inspect Ventures Fund, a seed capital fund.

CALM Chain offers three primary services:

  • Employer of Record outsourcing services

  • Executive search services

  • Human resources research services.

A breakdown of the revenue contribution from each of these service offering segments is shown in the pie chart below:

The company's client base includes

multinational companies and governmental organizations, regional branch offices in Hong Kong, financial institutions, construction companies, fintech company and other SMEs.

Talent Quest also maintains a website for job postings and a skills database to help match job openings with prospective personnel.

What is CALM Chain’s Market?

The company operates in the HR professional services market.

According to a 2026 market research report by Mordor Intelligence, the Asia Pacific region HR professional services was an estimated $134 billion in 2025 and was expected to exceed $203 billion by the end of 2030.

If it achieved this growth, would represent an 8.63% CAGR from 2026 to 2030, a moderate rate of growth for an already large industry.

CALM Chain operates in a subset of the industry and in one smaller region, Hong Kong.

The industry is adopting digital-oriented HR solutions, such as cloud-based HR management software, amid a growing demand for outsourced HR services, such as payroll processing, recruiting, and compliance.

The company faces intense competition from competitors of all sizes, including global full-service firms, boutique specialists, local Hong Kong agencies, and in-house corporate recruiting teams.

Also, sectors such as government and banking clients often have long tendering processes, which makes for higher barriers to entry in some cases.

The company's strengths include long-term relationships with certain clients, a proprietary candidate database, and a comprehensive suite of services.

Weaknesses include high client concentration and regional location concentration as well as reliance on a small number of key personnel in the company.

Its business opportunities include the potential to expand into Southeast Asian countries such as Vietnam, Malaysia, and Indonesia and integrating AI technologies to automate candidate matching to streamline administrative tasks.

Threats to CALM’s prospects include intense and varied market competition with generally low barriers to entry and an uncertain regulatory environment.

CALM Chain’s Recent Financial Results

CCIL’s revenue has grown materially from a small base, while it has begun to produce a profit on higher gross margin.

CALM Chain’s IPO Plan

CCIL intends to sell three million shares of Class A stock at a proposed midpoint price of $6.00 per share.

Immediately after the IPO, the firm’s enterprise value will approximate $111 million.

Dilution to new shareholders will be $5.46, and the percentage share float at IPO will be 15%.

Class A shareholders (the public) will have one vote per share.

Class B shareholder and company founder/Chairman Teh will have twenty (20) votes per share and will have voting control of the company immediately after the IPO, so the company will be a ‘controlled company’ according to Nasdaq’s rules.

Net proceeds from the IPO will be used by the company for:

approximately 30% for business development and expansion into the Asia Pacific market;

approximately 30% for applying artificial intelligence technology into the business for resources optimization; and

approximately 40% for general administration and working capital purposes.
